title: Bye-stake insertion
subtitle: How and when to insert bye-stakes to add density and rigidity to basket sides
excerpt: Bye-stakes are additional rods pushed into the side weaving after the upsett, doubling the uprights in the basket wall. They tighten the weave, strengthen the sides, and allow a more vertical profile on a wider basket. This technique tutorial covers selection, pointing, insertion sequence, and the tie-in method.
sourceNotes: Cooke The Basketmaker (1882) for the bye-stake insertion method and the double-stake border transition.

glossaryTerms.definition: An additional rod inserted alongside a main stake after the upsett, to increase weave density and side strength.
One of the upright structural rods forming the sides of a basket.
The first waling rows at the base of the sides that pull the stakes upright and establish the basket shape.
A two-rod weave where two weavers alternate in front of one stake and behind the next. Finer and more even than a rand.
A three-rod weave that gives structural strength to basket sides.

content.text: A single set of stakes leaves gaps between uprights that suit a light basket but are too open for a market or log basket. Inserting
bye-stakes
after the
upsett
halves that spacing and makes the wall denser and stronger. The extra uprights also help keep a vertical profile as the sides grow.
When to use bye-stakes

Insert bye-stakes when the basket will carry over 3 kg. Also use them when the base is wider than 250 mm and single-stake gaps would be too open, or when the design needs a dense side for a lid or clips, or when the border weave needs extra uprights to work cleanly.
Selecting and preparing bye-stakes
Bye-stakes should be the same thickness as the main
stakes

or slightly thinner. Cut them to a length 30 to 40 mm shorter than the main stakes; this ensures the bye-stake tops finish below the border level and do not need to be woven into the border. Point each bye-stake with the secateurs: two diagonal cuts to form a fine tapered tip 20 mm long. Soak the bye-stakes alongside the main basket material.
Insertion sequence
Insert bye-stakes after the upsett
waling

rows are complete and before the rand or pairing weave begins. Use the bodkin to open a path in the waling rows immediately to the left of each main stake. Push the pointed bye-stake down into this gap, driving it alongside the main stake for 40 to 50 mm into the waling and down to the base level. The bye-stake butt should sit firmly in the upsett rows; if it can be pulled back out easily, push it deeper.
Insert every alternate stake first, check the spacing is even, then insert the remaining ones. This gives you a chance to correct uneven spacing before all uprights are in place.
Tying in
Once all bye-stakes are inserted, run two rows of
pairing

weave over both the main stakes and the bye-stakes together. This locks the bye-stakes in position before the main side weave begins. Without the tie-in rows, the bye-stakes will migrate upward as the weaving progresses.
Common mistakes
Inserting bye-stakes before the upsett is complete: the waling rows must be in place first to give the bye-stake butt something to grip. Pre-upsett insertion produces floppy, poorly anchored stakes.
Cutting bye-stakes the same length as main stakes: bye-stake tops should finish below the border to avoid confusion in the border weave. Mark the bye-stakes before insertion if necessary.
Omitting the tie-in pairing rows after insertion: without them, bye-stakes shift during weaving and the side becomes uneven. Work two rows of pairing over all uprights right after insertion.
